description: The original, the classic. The one that started it all.
The ideal response to any PR opened right before lunch.
You didn't read it. Nobody reads it. It's fine.
Approved, but with a knowing sigh.
When the code is so bad that only divine intervention will save it.
A playful reinterpretation used humorously.
No notes. Just vibes. Shipping it.
Business-driven development at its finest.
A beautiful metaphor for collaboration and teamwork.
An accurate description of how most code gets written.
Spiritual approval for a PR you don't fully understand.
Only the most senior engineers know this truth.
CI passed. That's basically the same as a human review, right?
Your local environment is a crime scene, but ship it anyway.
Time-honored tradition in open source.
Management approved it in a deck. The engineers weep.
Somehow more productive than most sprint planning sessions.
The most honest description of async code review.
Not strictly a code review term, but applicable.
The code style is immaculate. The test coverage is zero.
Honesty unlocked.
When someone writes really beautiful code and you want them to know.
The corporate reflex. Problem solved via calendar invite.
That function is 800 lines. Nobody will say anything. LGTM.
Surfboard not included. The code is still a wipeout.
Optimism before the deploy. Chaos after.
You've been debugging for six hours. The sun has set. LGTM.
A workflow embraced by more engineers than will admit it.
A rallying cry for data scientists who've touched the configs.
Best code is written in a chaotic fugue state. This is that.
Pure encouragement. No notes. Just positivity.
The control flow is a fever dream. Approved.
Reserved for code so transcendent it breaks the rating scale.
A gentle reminder that perfection is the enemy of shipping.
When the main branch is already on fire, one more won't hurt.
Someone will laugh at this code in 2 years. Probably you.
Modern code review: delegate to the machine, merge the answer.
It works. That's enough. That's the bar. Cleared.
This codebase predates the company. Nobody knows what it does. It must be preserved.
Three espresso shots deep. The PR review is caffeinated.
Pass the burden to the most experienced. They'll know what to do. Maybe.
You merged your first PR. This is what success feels like.
The bar is low and this code cleared it. A win is a win.
This is above my pay grade. Escalating.
Collective approval is still approval. Democracy in code review.
The merge conflicts were resolved... somehow.
A scroll and a click. Peer review, done.
Every merged PR is a small victory. Celebrate each one.
From the deck that got funding. The code tells a different story.
Self-reviewing your own PR and being genuinely proud of it.
Impossible. But somehow it happened. LGTM.
We are but particles in the quantum foam of the CI pipeline.
The eternal cycle of every software project.
The standup when someone shows the production metrics.
The real reason Friday deploys get skipped.
Started with bash. Now it's a distributed system. Classic.
The codebase opened and something happened. Nobody knows.
The product still has bugs but the campaign goes live Monday.
No standards, no docs, one brave engineer holding it together.
Every refactor reveals another layer of decisions made in despair.
Unplug everything. Walk away. Come back Monday.
The GitHub notification was dismissed without opening the diff.
Someone actually read the RFC before writing the code. Miraculous.
All checks passed. There are no checks. Ship it.
A rare act of gratitude towards the people who keep the lights on.
Like legacy code: layer upon layer, nobody remembers why.
A radical act of optimism in the enterprise software industry.
eslint-disable on every line. The linter weeps silently.
The author left the company. The PR remains. Nobody dares close it.
Infrastructure as code becomes infrastructure as chaos.
No further discussion required. The deadline was yesterday.
Eighty percent coverage... of the happy path only.
The bundle weighs 4MB. The landing page loads in 9 seconds. Ship it.
The architecture doc promised elegance. The implementation delivered entropy.
Friday afternoon merge. Full radio silence until the post-incident review.
Ctrl+F for your name. Not found. LGTM.
Half the data came over. Nobody checked which half.
The best review is one that builds trust as much as it catches bugs.
The variable name was unhinged. It stays. The build passes.
The code works by accident. Do not refactor. Do not touch. Do not look directly at it.
Nobody wrote this code. Nobody owns it. It simply exists.
The PR review chat had 200 messages. One brave soul signed off and left.
Famous last words before a production incident.
The whole product is a Jira template duct-taped to a Lambda.
The codebase has no comments. The reviewer has no context. Approved.
Every bug is a learning opportunity. Some opportunities are on-call.
P99 is 14 seconds. The SLA says 200ms. Ship it anyway.
Sometimes the best response to a PR is stepping away for a brew.
Full awareness of the consequences. Zero hesitation.
The project was deprecated the next morning. This was the final commit.
The diff is 4,000 lines. You need both screens and a prayer.
Uncertainty dressed up as confidence. A classic.
When the code is so clean it reads like poetry. This happens roughly once per career.
A rallying cry. The whole team ships together.
The ticket exists. The problem persists. The sprint advances.
Nobody noticed how elegant that solution was. It will be overwritten in six months.
The review comment that should appear on every PR but never does.
The license is ambiguous. Legal is on vacation. We ship.
No variable names make sense. The function has no return type. We proceed.
Entry number 100. The milestone. The legend. The one.
The migration has 47 steps. Step 23 has never worked in production.
The feature reduces costs by 0.3%. It ships immediately.
The four pillars of a healthy engineering culture, in one comment.
Branch opened six months ago. Rebased three times. Still not merged.
The self-approval special. No review required when you trust your own judgment unconditionally.
The code is fine. The alignment meeting is not optional.
The senior engineer's mantra. Nobody fully understands the system. We keep going.
When someone's joke actually lands during code review.
Acknowledging another calendar invite with quiet resignation.
When the business logic defies all known laws of computation.
You approved it but your finger hovered over the button for three minutes.
Everything is a sprint. Everything is a marathon. Nothing is either.
141 warnings about trailing spaces. Each one personal.
Stepping away from the keyboard before you make things worse.
For the teammate who turns every sprint into a highlight reel.
Nothing illegal. Just everything wrong.
The most productive suggestion made all sprint.
That function that everyone is afraid to touch but nobody removes.
The review where you read the title and called it a day.
You cannot explain the algorithm. It works. Move on.
Performance review: optimistic at best.
What good teams are actually built on.
Enthusiasm over caution. The PR is three days old.
Untitled Goose Programmer. It honks and it ships.
Three levels of symlinks and you still haven't found the entry point.
Everything is under control. This is fine. Everything is fire.
The roadmap slide that always shows up in Q4.
You hit Merge at 4:59 PM on a Friday. Good luck.
When the standup runs forty minutes over because of 'context sharing'.
You miss the greenfield project you never got to build.
TypeScript gymnastics that should not compile but somehow does.
The CI is green. You have no idea why. Ship it.
Every debugging session begins with hope and ends with existential dread.
Staging works. That's basically the same thing, right?
The rebase that untangled six months of merge conflicts in one command.
Your dev environment has entered a state that requires formatting the laptop.
For the code review that changed how you think about the problem.
The PR message that turns a slow sprint into a productive one.
Nested for-loops in a language that has map() and filter().
A well-timed joke in the PR thread that defuses the tension.
The contract says 'best effort delivery'. It is delivering chaos.
Dashboard proliferation. Every number green. No one knows what they measure.
Intermittent test failures that only happen in CI on Wednesdays.
RTFM, said kindly, in a code review comment.
The ticket was opened in 2019. It's labelled 'quick win'.
P99: 2.3 seconds. P50: fine. Sleep: none.
Nobody wants to touch the visualization layer. Someone has to.
PR body: one meme, no description, three approvals.
You have been 'learning Rust' for four years.
The deployment script that overwrites prod with staging when run backwards.
You saw the first line. That's usually enough.
The commit that solves three open issues with four lines of code.
The decision that needs sign-off from seven stakeholders for a config change.
The pair programming session that makes you remember why you write code.
Zero-downtime deployment on a system that was never designed for it.
One commit: 'stuff'. 847 changed files.
Simple. Genuine. Works every time.
The event loop that somehow handles everything and panics about nothing.
CSS specificity reaching triple digits.
At some point, the architecture diagram starts to make sense.
Unit tests that mock everything including the actual unit under test.
At 40% zoom the code looks fine. That's your review.
TODO comments from three developers who have since left the company.
The architecture migration that touched every service and somehow didn't break production.
The legacy system that will outlive every person in this meeting.
A retrospective that actually changes something.
The PR that passed review because everyone was too tired to argue.
The function is 400 lines long. It only does one thing. Sort of.
Commenting on a stalled PR to get it moving again.
Proper mutex usage in a concurrent system. Applause is warranted.
Ambitious sprint planning that ignores velocity entirely.
A branch created in hope, never merged, never deleted.
The productivity dashboard that turned code review into a leaderboard.
Another database schema change. Another round of altered tables.
Performance is poor. Coverage is zero. Ship it.
A sincere comment that makes the PR author's whole week.
The only appropriate response to reading your own code from two years ago.
Q3 OKRs that assumed the laws of physics were optional.
You saw the diff size. You saw the test count. You approved anyway.
The code review that becomes a teaching moment both ways.
The idea in the tech debt backlog that nobody champions but everyone knows is right.
Flexbox migration from an ancient float-based layout. Courage required.
The feature request that made it to three sprints before budget cuts.
The deploy pipeline at 5 PM on a release day.
The polite way to say you've been waiting for this approval for a week.
The bug fix that accidentally fixed another, undocumented bug at the same time.
A codebase that, despite everything, people are proud to work in.
The refactor that started as a 'quick cleanup' and is now in its third month.
Caching a calculation that runs 4ms. The meeting to discuss it ran 90 minutes.
The commit message that says 'fix' for a 1,200-line change.
Not your best work. You know it. They know it. Merge anyway.
Adding instrumentation before adding the feature it's supposed to measure.
The developer who defends the monolith at every architecture review. Correctly.
The quiet act of staring at a blank terminal before opening a third Stack Overflow tab.
The comment that solves your problem, teaches you something new, and makes you laugh.
Sprint nine of a twelve-sprint 'temporary' crunch period.
Writing tests for a module nobody touches. Future you will be grateful.
The ritual of untangling circular imports on a Monday morning.
The slide deck version of the feature, resplendent in clip art.
Deleting a feature you built with love because the data said no.
Celebration is mandatory. The milestone was moving a date.
Tech debt accumulates with the same inevitability as entropy.
Your reaction when the PR description says: 'minor refactor'.
The architecture discussion that devolves into font preferences within six minutes.
The codebase that works but can't be explained by anyone currently employed.
The senior dev who turns every PR into a curriculum.
The AI-written PR description that tells you less than the diff.
The engineer who reviewed your PR last week is no longer at the company.
Recursion chosen because it looked elegant. Stack overflow incoming.
The refactor nobody wanted to do that halved load times six months later.
The one-liner that replaces forty lines because you finally read the stdlib docs.
Gantt chart has entered the chat. Meeting is now two hours.
Forty lines of mock setup for a test that asserts one boolean.
You named a variable 'data'. You named the other one 'data2'.
The regex that passes all tests and fails on every real input.
A gentle nudge to unblock the PR that has sat in review for a week.
Velocity is down 40%. The dashboard is still green. Nobody panic.
The known bug that shipped because removing it introduced a worse bug.
Coverage went from 0% to 1%. Progress.
The 'base controller' class that has 87 methods and no clear purpose.
The library built by one person over two years that quietly powers half your stack.
The server during the one sale your company runs each year.
The Haskell pull request nobody was expecting in a Python codebase.
When someone makes a joke during standup that actually improves the mood.
The interface that is documented with one example that doesn't cover your case.
The design system that makes every page feel like it was built with intention.
Floating-point arithmetic where 0.1 + 0.2 is close enough for government work.
Distributed tracing reveals the request bounced through eleven services. Six are yours.
Deleting code you're proud of because simplicity serves better than cleverness.
The TypeScript `as unknown as SomeType` cast that is the load-bearing wall of the app.
The heroic commitment to dependency updates on a Friday afternoon.
Pair programming where the senior engineer asks more questions than they answer.
The script nobody owns but everyone depends on, living in a shared S3 bucket.
Shipped to prod. Slack notifications: silent. Monitoring: green. You feel nothing but dread.
Reading a PR description that begins 'So this is going to sound bad, but'.
The TODOs that name the original author. They left four years ago.
The Python script about to learn what the GIL actually does.
The live demo where everything should have failed and somehow didn't.
Standardizing on one language, one framework, one ORM. Hire accordingly.
An open-source project with a changelog that reads like a letter to its users.
The refactor that removed more lines than it added and solved more bugs than it created.
Still debugging at 2 AM. The error is a missing semicolon.
The comment that turns a stalled PR into a merged PR in under an hour.
The conditional approval: 'Ship if you're comfortable. I'm not reviewing again.'
The feature flag that gates a brilliant idea that never got turned on.
The most optimistic interpretation of a Monday standup.
The team that ships a platform rewrite without a single regression in production.
Joined for the culture deck, stayed for the perpetual on-call rota.
Reading git blame to understand why the code exists as it does.
The moment you realize you understand the codebase better than its original authors.
